data.title: Deutsche Bank: Recent Pullback Provides A Buying Opportunity
data.description:
Introduction

Deutsche Bank Aktiengesellschaft (DB) shares are off to a weak start in 2026, impacted by private credit exposures and the expected effects from the Iran war, which come against the backdrop of the ongoing conflict in Ukraine. While the bar for taking profits after DB's 2025 share price surge is not particularly high, the recent rout has restored DB's margin of safety, thus providing a buying opportunity for opportunistic investors.

I confirm my previous Buy rating on DB, with my bullish outlook underpinned by:

  • An undemanding 8.3x trailing P/E multiple, representing a circa 23% discount relative to the broader European banking sector.
  • Ambitious 2028 financial targets, potentially allowing for significant EPS growth if downside risks do not materialize.
  • A healthy 14.2% CET1 capital ratio and recently confirmed 2026 outlook, indicating little need to raise liquidity amid investor scrutiny of DB's private credit exposure.

Near-Term Headwinds

Most recently, DB came into news headlines regarding its €25.9 billion private credit exposure. In its 2025 annual report, DB elaborates:

Approximately 73% of this exposure is to multi-asset Lender facilities (ABS) collateralized by highly diversified mid-market corporate loans in the U.S. and the EU, across industry sectors, with conservative advance rates of ~65% and almost entirely investment grade rated. The remainder is diversified across single and multi asset lenders Net Asset Value (NAV) Financing, Single Asset Financing, non-bank CRE lending, business development companies (BDC) and subscription finance.

I should highlight that alongside its annual report publication, DB also confirmed its outlook for 2026, which most notably includes the assumption of a slight decline in the provisioning for credit losses. This is quite encouraging, as the statement comes after the outbreak of the war in Iran and increased market scrutiny on private credit markets.

In its annual reporting, DB does not explicitly break down its Middle East loan exposure. As such, assessing direct downside risks from the war in Iran is quite difficult. Looking at employee counts, however, Saudi Arabia stands at 52 and Qatar at only 4, which relative to the 95,980 headcount for DB as a whole is negligible.

To sum up, while better Middle East disclosure would be welcome in future reports, as things stand, DB's direct downside risks from the Iran war appear quite limited. As such, the bank's private credit exposure is a key near-term risk factor to watch. The good news is that with a CET1 ratio of 14.2% (above its 13.5-14.0% objective for 2028), DB is in no rush to raise liquidity. This is quite good, as current stress surrounding private credit appears to be focused more on the inability for investors to access their funds rather than underlying portfolio quality issues.

Medium-Term Opportunity

Aside from near-term risk factors highlighted above, DB appears confident in reaching a higher profitability in the next few years. In numbers, the bank expects to deliver a return on tangible equity (ROTE) of >13% by 2028, a significant improvement relative to the ROTE of 10.3% achieved in 2025. This is somewhat above the 12% ROTE I expected in my previous coverage of DB.

Where are the gains expected to come from? Firstly, as discussed in the presentation of CFO Raja Akram, DB aims to achieve better capital efficiency and a cost-income ratio of below 60% in 2028, allowing more of the anticipated 5% annual revenue growth to reach DB's bottom line.

If you look at European peers, a cost-income ratio of 60% is still quite high, indicating that even if DB manages to reach its targets, it would still rank as one of Europe's less efficient banks. For comparison, UniCredit (UNCFF) achieved a 38% cost/income ratio in 2025.

What are the key assumptions underpinning the 2028 profitability improvement? Well, DB has penciled in a 2.00% ECB deposit rate in 2026, gradually increasing to 2.5% in 2028. The bank also anticipated 1.1% GDP expansion in the Eurozone this year, jumping to 1.3% in 2028.

Against the backdrop of recent events, the assumptions regarding ECB policy appear reasonable (although I would note that if nothing changes, odds are for somewhat higher ECB rates in 2026 but lower in 2027-2028 as the effect of the energy price surge fades). European GDP growth, however, will likely be below DB's expectations, weighed down by high-energy costs and no end in sight for the war in Ukraine.

To sum up, on balance I see risks for DB's >13% ROTE target as tilted to the downside.

Valuation And Rating

While DB stock has naturally been quite volatile in recent weeks, as I write this, the shares trade at €25.61/share, representing an 8.3x trailing P/E multiple and a tangible book value discount of around 17%. This remains notably cheaper than direct European peers held in the iShares STOXX Europe 600 Banks UCITS ETF (DJXSF), which trade at roughly 10.83x their trailing earnings.

In light of DB's significant expected earnings growth and recently confirmed 2026 outlook, the circa 23% trailing P/E discount relative to peers appears excessive. The good news is that DB started its €1 billion share buyback on February 28, indicating the bank will be able to take advantage of the recent pullback in the share price. Investors can track progress and achieved share buyback prices on the DB website here. I estimate that the buyback will be enough to retire almost 2% of shares outstanding, providing a small boost to tangible book value thanks to the current discount.

Overall, I would say that a lot of gloom is currently reflected in DB shares. Hence, even if the bank ultimately underperforms its 2026 outlook and reports a higher cost of risk, the profitability hit is arguably priced in to an extent. As such, notwithstanding risks that I will discuss in the next section, I confirm my previous Buy rating on the shares.

Risks

Amid ongoing political tensions, weaker-than-expected GDP growth (associated with higher credit costs) is a key risk to consider. After several years of lackluster economic growth, the IMF had penciled in a 1.1% expansion in German GDP this year, accelerating to 1.5% in 2027. This will likely be revised down, potentially putting pressure on DB's provisioning.

While financial market volatility is arguably good for DB's trading business in fixed income and currencies, lower valuations will likely weigh on asset management fees collected by DB. Since DB targets a 2028 ROTE of >40% in asset management, persistently low stock and bond valuations (i.e., high fixed income yields) can also impact returns, thus undermining a key pillar of the bank's future profitability improvement.

Elevated uncertainty can also weigh on loan demand, leading DB to undershoot its 2% annual loan growth target in 2026-2028. This in turn could lead to the bank underperforming its net interest income growth expectations, resulting in a higher-than-expected cost/income ratio in 2028.

Conclusion

After a stellar 2025 for DB stock, 2026 has so far been characterized by profit taking, driven by DB's elevated private credit exposure and broader economic uncertainty.

Against this backdrop, it is encouraging to see DB confirming its 2026 targets, potentially indicating that the cost of risk should remain low. At the same time, ongoing share repurchases allow the bank to take advantage of share price weakness, potentially retiring almost 2% of shares outstanding.

While I see no DB-specific near-term catalysts for the share price, a resolution to the war in Iran and/or Ukraine could improve risk sentiment. This scenario is so far neglected by the market, allowing opportunistic investors to enter DB at a circa 23% discount to the broader European banking sector.

Key risks in the investment case include higher-than-expected credit loss provisioning, underperformance in the asset management segment, and lower-than-projected loan demand.

Thank you for reading.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Adobe: Record Quarter, But ARR Slowdown And CEO Transition Drag Stock Down
data.description:
Thesis Summary

Adobe Inc. (ADBE) just delivered a record quarter, beating revenue and earnings expectations while showing increased traction across its AI initiatives.

And yet the stock has sold off, down over 6% as I write this.

It seems like investors appear concerned about a slowdown in ARR growth and potentially the surprise announcement that CEO Shantanu Narayen will step down.

And yet, I see this as an opportunity for those willing to weather the storm.

AI is a threat to Adobe, but it is also an opportunity, and the company is making the right moves to become a long-term winner.

Interestingly, legendary investor Michael Burry apparently has initiated a position in Adobe. Burry likes to be a contrarian, and that’s certainly the case with Adobe right now.

Is Adobe the new PayPal (PYPL) or the next Alphabet (GOOGL)? This is what I asked in my last article, and the stock is down 27% since then.

But in the face of a record quarter and Michael Burry’s endorsement, I remain bullish.

A Strong Quarter That Somehow Disappointed

Adobe’s Q1 results were objectively solid.

The company achieved $6.4B in revenue in Q1, which represents a 12% YoY growth. On the earnings front, non-GAAP EPS came in at $6.06. This was actually a double beat by the company.

Profitability also remained very strong, with non-GAAP operating margins reaching 47.4%.

Yet the stock sold off after the earnings release.

The way most see it, this is likely due to two key reasons: the slowdown in ARR growth and the CEO's departure.

The knee-jerk reaction of the stock is often to trade off of the ARR figure, a metric that is rarely-if-ever disclosed by mega-cap software companies, and investors often struggle when they assess this metric,

Source: JPM Analyst, SA Press Release.

Now moving on to the CEO, leadership changes always introduce uncertainty, especially when the departing executive has overseen such a successful era.

During the call, Narayen acknowledged the transition while emphasizing the company’s continued focus on growth.

I will continue to lead Adobe as CEO and will stay on as Chair of the Board to support my successor.

Source: Earnings Call.

But as mentioned above, Narayen will remain chairman and assist with the transition, which reduces the risk of significant disruption.

The AI Narrative Is Being Misunderstood

The basic concern around Adobe is that generative AI could reduce the need for traditional creative software.

This is just one of many AI disruptions that the market is exaggerating, in my opinion.

Like many other SaaS companies, Adobe is not sitting on the sidelines watching this transformation happen.

Instead, the company is embedding AI directly into its products through its Firefly generative AI platform.

And message metrics suggest this strategy is working.

Generative credit consumption increased more than 45% quarter-over-quarter.

Source: Earnings Call.

Even more importantly, AI usage is moving beyond experimentation into real production workflows.

Video generative actions grew more than 8x year-over-year and audio generative actions doubled year-over-year.

Source: Earnings Call.

Clearly, AI is becoming integrated into the creative process, but it's not replacing it. If anything, this gives Adobe the chance to add more value to the final consumer.

The Freemium Strategy Is Masking Growth

What a lot of investors are also overlooking is Adobe’s expanding user base.

The company reported over 850 million monthly active users across its platforms, which represents roughly 17% YoY growth.

But as management explained, this surge in users is intentionally being driven through freemium distribution models.

These offerings have a near-term impact on ARR… but set the foundation for accelerated growth over time.

Source: Earnings Call.

This will be painful in the short term, but it’s the right approach to take in the face of AI disruption.

Valuation Is Compelling (Even for Burry)

And yet, the valuation just keeps sliding and looking increasingly attractive.

The company trades at roughly 11–12x forward earnings and a PEG below one.

This is unreasonable for a company still growing at double digits and with such strong margins.

Like with PayPal, it becomes a matter of sentiment right now, but eventually logic and pure cash flow should help turn Adobe’s price around.

Risks

But there are still plenty of potential risks with Adobe.

First and foremost, generative AI could still compress Adobe’s pricing power over time.

As AI tools become commoditized or competitors offer similar tools at lower cost, Adobe’s historically strong margins could face pressure.

Secondly, with the company currently transitioning toward a freemium user acquisition model, this delays monetization, and if Adobe struggles to convert its rapidly growing user base into paying subscribers, this could be a problem

Final Thoughts

The market hates Adobe right now, but it has found some love in Michael Burry. Is the legendary investor wrong, or just early again?

Personally, I think at this price, Adobe is a compelling Buy, even with the risks presented by AI.

data.title: Adobe: Fear Of A Value Trap
data.description:
Shares of Adobe (ADBE) have plunged to levels not seen since 2018, some eight years ago, as a testament to the degree of turmoil that AI has caused in the land of software. The news about long-time CEO Shantanu Narayen resigning and an unconvincing outlook, with no quick fixes to address the AI scare, triggered a new selling wave. Currently, shares are down about two-thirds from the peak in 2021.

The question is if this is really the transformative and bloodshed moment or if further dips will be seen. The pace of creative destruction is so rapid, yet it's hardly seen in the results. This creates compelling valuation multiples.

This raises the question about the degree and pace of disruption in relation to the current valuations, but fear about a rapid disruption raises the bar to get involved as well. I'm usually attracted to large pullbacks, some of which are covered in greater detail at Value In Corporate Events, but I'm holding back on this one.

The News

Other than highly anticipated first-quarter results, investors in Adobe were mostly digesting the news that Narayen, who served as Adobe's CEO for some 18 years, will resign after a successor is appointed. That news was quite unexpected, certainly given the challenges, or at least perceived challenges, faced by the business.

Looking at the results, the company reported a 12% increase in first-quarter revenues to $6.40 billion, 97% comprised of subscription services, complemented by tiny service and product revenue streams.

Subscription revenues rose by some 13%, as total revenues rose by 11% on a constant currency basis. About 70% of sales of the business are generated from creative and marketing professionals, growing 11% in constant currency terms, while the remaining business professional and consumer revenues were up as much as 15% on the same basis.

While the company reported an AI-first ARR that more than tripled over the year, no quantification of these numbers was announced. Adobe guided for modest sequential revenue growth, seeing second-quarter sales between $6.43 and $6.48 billion.

The company reported incredible profits with GAAP earnings of $1.9 billion, translating into GAAP earnings of $4.60 per share. While adjusted earnings are reported at $6.06 per share, that excludes a $1.25 per share stock-based compensation expense, which I do not like to adjust for. Somewhat concerning in the outlook is that despite sequential revenue growth, the same does not apply to earnings. GAAP earnings are seen between $4.35-$4.40 per share, with adjusted earnings seen between $5.80-$5.85 per share.

The company ended the quarter with $662 million in net cash despite $2.5 billion in buybacks over the quarter, used to retire 8.1 million shares at an average price of just over $300 per share. Likely a modest net debt load will be incurred going forward, given that Adobe has a pending deal to acquire Semrush, a $1.9 billion deal announced in November, as I expect a continuation of buybacks this quarter.

Valuations Implode

With 411 million shares now trading around the $250 mark, the market value of Adobe has effectively imploded to $100 billion, for a pretty similar enterprise valuation, as discussed above. This values the business at just 4 times annualized sales. GAAP earnings trend at $17-$18 per share, or could be seen a bit higher if we back out amortization charges, making the shares trade at 12 times GAAP earnings.

These are very cheap valuations, far cheaper than in the past, as Adobe, by my math, spent about $50 billion to acquire shares back over the past decade. This was enough to reduce the share count by nearly 100 million shares, on average having bought shares at much higher levels.

What Now?

The truth is that Adobe has been early in the buybacks, but fortunately, it managed balance sheet integrity on a net basis by being unleveraged. Currently, reported sales numbers look decent, yet investors are not just fearful about competition from cheaper and nimbler competitors like Figma and Canva; it really is the concerns around AI, as it appears that switching barriers are coming down as well.

While the company claims that new user numbers are gaining momentum, this is seen in growing freemium accounts. After all, a 50% increase in freemium MAU to 80 million looks impressive across various product suites. That suggests that ARPU is coming down, as upselling here will be very tough. The company furthermore reported an 850 million user base of its entire suite of products, which is up 17% on the year before.

This suggests that many users are using the products for free. In fact, if we apply total revenues to user numbers, this makes the ARPU just $7.50 per quarter, as a testament to the massive dispersion between total users and total paying user numbers.

The question is how quickly the disruption will happen, with second-quarter sales seen up 10%, although earnings will see some impact. The question of where this margin pressure comes from was not really discussed on the conference call. The market is clearly not convinced, and that creates a very interesting situation.

To be very bearish on the stock is very late in my view, down two-thirds from the highs, for a current industry leader operating with a strong balance sheet, still projecting growth, and trading at just about 12-13 times earnings. This looks too late to be bearish, as the real question is if now is the time to get involved on the long side.

While the numbers suggest a no-brainer, the market is clearly pricing in growth ending or reversing in the medium to long term. Other than the questions about disruption, there's an equally important question of the degree and pace at which this will happen. This is arguably key here, with the jury still out here.

Hence, I'm very interested given the valuation discussions, but I fear buying into the value trap, which raises the bar to get involved. I'm hoping to see another layer of peak pessimism to get somewhat of a better buffer for such outcomes.

data.title: Four Years In: What CTA Has Historically Delivered For Investor Portfolios
data.description:
March 2026 marks a milestone worth noting: the Simplify Managed Futures Strategy ETF (CTA) hits its four-year track record. In a market environment that has tested nearly every assumption in traditional portfolio construction, that's a meaningful moment to take stock of what the strategy was built to do and whether it delivered.

And deliver, it has! From inception on March 7, 2022 through February 28, 2026, CTA has delivered investors:

  • 11%+ annualized NAV return
  • Negative correlation to both stocks and bonds
  • Diversification, when it matters most

The performance data quoted represents past performance and is no guarantee of future results. Current performance may be lower or higher than the performance data quoted. Investment returns and the principal value of an investment will fluctuate so that an investor’s shares, when redeemed, may be worth more or less than their original cost. For performance data current to the most recent month-end please call (855) 772-8488 or go to simplify.us/etfs. For standardized performance, go to simplify.us/etfs/cta-simplify-managed-futures-strategy-etf.

Diversifiers are often cast aside for their inability to boost portfolio returns. Sure, they diversified, but if that’s all they did, they often feel like opportunity costs as investors seek return! We believe CTA helped to provide both returns and diversification benefits. The “Have your Cake and Eat it Too” type of solution that historically delivered diversification with attractive absolute returns.

The Problem CTA Was Designed to Solve

The post-2020 macro landscape hasn't been kind to the classic 60/40 portfolio. Stock-bond correlations have turned positive, inflation has proven stickier than expected, and the strategy that investors relied on for decades has eroded significantly. When both stocks and bonds sell off at the same time (does 2022 ring a bell?), investors are left without a true hedge.

CTA was built specifically for this environment.

The fund seeks to deliver three things:

  1. Diversification in the form of low correlation to equities
  2. Attractive absolute returns
  3. Positive relative returns in risk-off environments

It does this by dynamically adjusting between long and short positions across 50+ commodity and interest rate futures markets daily, using institutional-grade algorithms developed by the hedge fund provider, Altis Partners.

What the Four-Year Track Record Shows

The numbers tell a compelling story.

CTA has delivered strong absolute returns. From inception through 02/28/26, CTA's NAV total cumulative return was 51.52%, translating to an annualized return of 11.01%. For context, the SG CTA Index, the benchmark for the managed futures category, returned just 3.16% annualized over the same period.

A more commonly referenced benchmark for adding alternatives is often the 60/40 portfolio, which CTA has also outperformed annually by +1.42% - showcasing that it is not only a strong portfolio diversifier with negative correlation, but it can also help to improve total portfolio performance.

Figure 1: Total Returns: CTA, SG CTA Index, and 60/40 Portfolio (03/07/22 - 02/28/26)

Adding CTA improved portfolio outcomes across the board. A portfolio that replaced 10% of core bonds in a 60/40 with CTA (60% S&P 500 Index / 30% Bloomberg US Aggregate Bond Index/ 10% CTA) outperformed the traditional benchmark on every key metric over the four-year period:

Figure 2: Key Statistics (03/07/22 - 02/28/26)

More return, less volatility, and a shallower maximum drawdown. That's a rare combination in portfolio construction with such a small allocation tweak.

The correlation profile is genuinely differentiated. Since inception, CTA has maintained a -0.10 correlation to the S&P 500 Index and -0.36 correlation to the Bloomberg US Aggregate Bond Index. In an environment where stocks and bonds themselves have shown a +0.17 correlation to each other, CTA has served as a true diversifier, not just in theory, but in practice.

Figure 3: Correlations (03/07/22 - 02/28/26)

CTA hedged when it mattered most. An asymmetric return profile—better downside mitigation without sacrificing upside—is exactly what the strategy was designed to deliver.

  • In months when the S&P 500 Index had a negative return, CTA posted an average +2.40% return.
  • In months when the S&P 500 Index had a positive return, CTA also had a positive return, averaging +0.39%.

Source: Bloomberg. Data from 03/07/22 through 02/28/26. Traditional 60/40 portfolio = 60% S&P 500 Index, 40% Bloomberg US Aggregate Bond Index.

What Makes CTA Different

Not all managed futures strategies are built alike. CTA's differentiated process combines a core trend-following model with additional signals designed to enhance diversification, increase nimbleness, optimize position sizing, and aim for reduced drawdown risk. The models are evaluated and can be repositioned daily, allowing the strategy to adapt to shifting market regimes in real time.

The result is institutional-caliber execution—powered by Altis Partners' hedge fund algorithms—delivered in a daily liquid ETF with full transparency at a 0.75% gross expense ratio.

Four Years In, the Case Remains Strong

The macro backdrop that made CTA relevant in 2022 hasn't gone away. Positive stock-bond correlations, elevated inflation sensitivity, and geopolitical uncertainty aren't going anywhere quickly. For advisors building portfolios designed to hold up across regimes—not just in bull markets—the four-year track record of CTA makes a strong case for a dedicated managed futures allocation.

The data has spoken. The diversification worked. The returns were there. And the strategy did what it said it would do when it mattered most.

Glossary

Annualized Volatility: Measures how much a stock price varies over one year.

Bloomberg US Aggregate Bond Index: A broad-based flagship benchmark that measures the investment grade, US dollar-denominated, fixed- rate taxable bond market. The index includes Treasuries, government-related and corporate securities, MBS (agency fixed-rate pass-throughs), ABS and CMBS (agency and non-agency).

Bloomberg US EQ:FI 60:40 Index (BBg 60/40): Designed to measure cross-asset market performance in the US. The index rebalances monthly to 60% equities and 40% fixed income. The equity and fixed income allocation is represented by Bloomberg US Large Cap (B500T) and Bloomberg US Agg (LBUSTRUU) respectively.

Bloomberg US Large Cap Index: A float-market-cap-weighted benchmark tracking the performance of the 500 most highly capitalized, investable companies in the US equity market. It acts as a comprehensive, real-time indicator for the performance of large-sized US companies.

Futures: Derivative financial contracts that obligate parties to buy or sell an asset at a predetermined future date and price. The buyer must purchase or the seller must sell the underlying asset at the set price, regardless of the current market price at the expiration date.

Futures Contract: A legal agreement to buy or sell a particular commodity asset, or security at a predetermined price at a specified time in the future. Futures contracts are standardized for quality and quantity to facilitate trading on a futures exchange.

Maximum Drawdown: The maximum observed loss from a peak to a trough of a portfolio, before a new peak is attained. Maximum drawdown is an indicator of downside risk over a specified time period.

S&P 500 Index: The index includes 500 leading U.S. large cap companies and captures approximately 80% coverage of the available market.

SG CTA Index (SocGen CTA): Provides the market with a reliable daily performance benchmark of major commodity trading advisors (CTAs). Calculates the net daily rate of return for a group of 20 CTAs selected from the largest managers open to new investment. The SG CTA Index is equal-weighted and reconstituted annually and has become recognized as the key managed futures performance benchmark.

Sharpe Ratio: The ratio compares the return of an investment with its risk. It’s a mathematical expression of the insight that excess returns over a period of time may signify more volatility and risk, rather than investing skill.

Investors should carefully consider the investment objectives, risks, charges, and expenses of Exchange Traded Funds (ETFs) before investing. To obtain an ETF's prospectus or Summary prospectus containing this and other important information, please call (855) 772-8488, or visit SimplifyETFs.com. Please read the prospectus carefully before you invest.

An investment in the fund involves risk, including possible loss of principal. Diversification does not ensure a profit or protect against a loss.

The fund is actively-managed and is subject to the risk that the strategy may not produce the intended results. The fund will also rely on the Futures Adviser’s judgments about the value and potential appreciation of particular securities which if assessed incorrectly could negatively affect the Fund.

The Fund’s use of futures may involve different or greater risks than investing directly in securities and the contract may not correlate perfectly with the underlying asset. These risks include leverage risk which means a small percentage of assets invested in futures can have a disproportionately large impact on the Fund. This risk could cause the Fund to lose more than the principal amount invested. Futures contracts may become mispriced or improperly valued when compared to the adviser’s expectation and may not produce the desired investment results. The Fund’s exposure to futures contracts is subject to risks related to rolling. Extended periods of contango or backwardation can cause significant losses for the Fund. Any short sales of the futures contracts by the fund theoretically involves unlimited loss potential since the market price of securities sold short may continuously increase.

Investments linked to commodity or currency futures contracts including exposure to non-U.S. currencies can be highly volatile affected by market movements, changes in interest rates or factors affecting a particular industry or commodity. Changes in currency exchange rates can be unpredictable or change quickly which will affect the value of the Fund.

Simplify ETFs are distributed by Foreside Financial Services, LLC. Foreside and Simplify are not related.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Village Farms: Positive Catalysts, But Caution Warranted For Now
data.description:
Village Farms International, Inc. (VFF) deserved caution after the stock zoomed last year from $1 to $3+. Now, the Canadian cannabis company faces a major test with the stock slumping back to $3 after Q4 results disappointed the market. My investment thesis remains Neutral on the stock, but the current support levels not holding is concerning for the short term.

Under The Surface

Village Farms reported the following Q4 '25 results leading to the weak stock action:

  • Q4 GAAP EPS of $0.01.
  • Revenue of $49.6M (+9.3% YoY).

The key to the story is that the cannabis company spent the last year transitioning out of the produce business. The cannabis portion of the business reported strong growth in the year.

In the Q4 '25 earnings release, CEO Michael DeGiglio highlighted the strong business as follows:

We grew global cannabis sales by 17% year-over-year despite just a partial year of contributions from our expanding Netherlands business, and international exports increased more than sixfold as we continue to capitalize on our leadership position among the world’s largest EU-GMP certified cannabis operators. We’ve set a higher standard for our Company on the global stage, and are continuing to invest behind our proven teams with enhancements to our operating capabilities.

Village Farms reported the following strong financial metrics that just aren't normally present in the Canadian cannabis space:

  • Canadian cannabis sales increased 10% YoY; International export cannabis sales increased 384% YoY.
  • Record full-year consolidated net income from continuing ops of $21.0 million or $0.19 per share, adjusted EBITDA from continuing ops of $49.9 Million, and operating cash flow of $58.1 million.

The one major hiccup to cash flows in 2026 was the $16 million payment for 2025 Canadian income taxes in February. The stock selloff was likely related to news of the company now paying taxes after exhausting carryover tax losses.

Strong Growth Prospects

Down at $3, Village Farms isn't priced for a stock with adjusted EBITDA of $50 million and operating cash flow topping $58 million. The stock only has a fully diluted market cap of $420 million with the 13% dip following earnings based on 142 million shares outstanding.

The post-earnings dip doesn't really add up. Village Farms has substantial ability to expand the cannabis business via bringing online the Delta 1 & 2 facilities in British Columbia, along with additional growth opportunities in international export with new countries added in 1H '26.

The Delta 2 facility alone will increase cannabis production capacity by 33% with the addition of 40 metric tons of annual production. The Delta 1 facility can't convert to cannabis until the start of 2027 and will provide another major growth path in 2028.

The wild card has always been expansion into the U.S. with 2.2M sq. ft. of greenhouse assets in Texas. The company forecasts the space could provide over $400 million in revenue once cannabis is approved in the U.S. for interstate commerce.

To add to the deep value in the stock, Village Farms has a net cash balance of $53 million. The company repurchased ~2 million shares since the start of Q4.

The consensus analyst estimates forecast revenues of $251 million in 2026 and an increase to $294 million in 2027. The stock trades at about 11x EPS targets and just over 1x revenue targets.

Village Farms offers a strong value at the current price with all the assets still to convert to cannabis operations, including the Phase 2 facility in the Netherlands. Investors should use $3 as a key support level, but any dip below this support level could set up a much better entry point in the $1.50 to $2.50 range.

The biggest risk is that the Canadian cannabis market is highly competitive. Village Farms is in a strong position now, but the additional capacity could just contribute to lower prices. The stock action suggests the market wasn't prepared for the large Canadian income taxes in 2025 recently paid in 2026.

Takeaway

The key investor takeaway is that Village Farms is attractively priced now, but the stock weakness is concerning. Investors need to worry about trying to catch a falling knife with $3 not holding after a solid Q4 earnings report and strong growth catalysts for 2026 and into 2027.

data.title: Morningstar: Wonderful Price And At Least A Fair Business
data.description:
In December 2025, I wrote my last article about Morningstar, Inc. (MORN), and in the conclusion I wrote:

In my opinion, Morningstar can already be seen as undervalued and might be a solid “Buy” already. However, I see a high probability for the stock continuing to decline and reaching about $160 once again. I personally would wait a little longer and hope that Morningstar will decline below $200 and reach my preferred entry target.

Now, Morningstar is trading below $200, and in the last few weeks it also reached my preferred entry point of $160 and briefly declined even below $150. Compared to my last article, in which I wrote that Morningstar is already undervalued, the stock declined another 16% in value.

As you can read in my disclosure, I have not bought Morningstar so far – despite the stock being on my watchlist and having reached the preferred entry point. In the following article, I will explain why I stayed to the sidelines so far (and don’t see it as the perfect choice) despite the stock being most likely undervalued. I will look at the risks Morningstar is facing – especially due to AI – but also argue that these risks only apply to parts of the business and should not be overblown at this point.

Quarterly and Full-Year Results

Let’s start by looking at the fourth quarter results Morningstar reported on Feb. 11, 2026. Revenue still increased from $591.0 million in Q4/24 to $641.1 million in Q4/25 – resulting in 8.5% year-over-year growth. While the top line was growing at a solid pace, operating income declined 5.1% year-over-year from $168.2 million in the same quarter last year to $159.6 million in this quarter. Diluted net income per share increased from $2.71 in Q4/24 to $2.83 in Q4/25 – resulting in 4.4% bottom line growth.

When looking at full-year results for fiscal 2025, we see a similar picture. Revenue increased from $2,275 million in fiscal 2024 to $2,446 million in fiscal 2025 – resulting in 7.5% year-over-year bottom line growth. Operating income increased even 8.6% year-over-year from $484.8 million in the previous year to $526.6 million in fiscal 2025. Finally, diluted net income per share increased 3.4% from $8.58 in fiscal 2024 to $8.87 in fiscal 2025.

We certainly can’t state that these are terrible results, and Morningstar is still growing in the mid-to-high single digits. Quarterly revenue growth was slowing down over the last few years, and current revenue growth rates are clearly below the long-term average. Nevertheless, growth rates are not in free-fall, and 8.5% revenue growth is still solid.

Splitting Up the Business

One way to describe Morningstar’s business is by splitting it up into four different services. One part of the business is the credit ratings (which are reported under the Morningstar Credit segment), which can be seen as transaction-based revenues. Another part of Morningstar’s business - the indices, which are now only reported in the Corporate and All Other segment. The biggest part of the business is the licensed-based revenues (especially PitchBook and Morningstar Direct) and finally, we can look at fee-based revenues from its wealth management and retirement offerings (including Morningstar Wealth and Morningstar Retirement).

In the following paragraphs we will look at these four parts of the business individually and assess how big the risks are right now and whether the sell-off of Morningstar is justified or not. We start by looking at the index business.

Index

At this point, the index business probably is not playing a major role for Morningstar anymore, as it is only reported under “Corporate and All Other” and we therefore only find limited information. We know that Morningstar Indexes revenue increased modestly on an organic and reported basis, and full-year revenue was $87.7 million (up from $84.7 million in fiscal 2024). This is resulting in about 3.5% of total revenue.

Nevertheless, I would see this business as very stable and difficult to attack by GenAI applications. Providing accurate data is not as easy as it might seem – especially as most companies have to pay for getting accurate market information. It's not so difficult to build software or GenAI applications to display the data and compare data, but getting accurate data is more difficult. Additionally, investors probably want to rely on established players and brand names they trust, and therefore it seems likely Morningstar will continue to grow in this segment.

Credit Ratings

A second segment that is protected very well is the credit ratings business. I would make similar arguments as for the index business. AI could be used by the company in the rating process (and most likely it's already used) to assess information and provide scoring models.

However, this is not how ratings work. We're not just looking for the cheapest company that can offer a rating. Ratings have to do with trust, and the brand name is very important. A new company will have to prove over a long time it can provide high quality and good ratings before it's taken seriously – using GenAI for ratings alone certainly won’t be enough and Morningstar (as well as Moody’s Corporation (MCO) and S&P Global (SPGI)) can also use GenAI to improve the rating process.

The Morningstar Credit segment performed quite well in the last few quarters, and revenue increased 21.7% year-over-year to $354.4 million (and is making up 14.5% of total revenue). It's still a smaller part of the business but growing at a high pace, and adjusted operating income increased even 51.9% year-over-year to $114.8 million in fiscal 2025 (which results in almost 20% of the company’s total operating income). However, one problem remains: The credit rating business is rather cyclical, and we should not expect these high growth rates to last forever. We will see quarters again with much lower growth rates. But we should not fear GenAI – only Moody’s and S&P Global and Fitch as serious competitors.

Retirement and Wealth Management

A third segment is the wealth management and retirement business and here we can especially focus on the retirement business as this business is highly profitable for Morningstar. While it's only responsible for about 5.5% of total revenue, it generated 11.6% of total operating income. The segment has an operating margin around 50% and grew its top line 8.3% in fiscal 2025.

In theory, such a service could easily be replaced by other companies, and especially GenAI could be helpful to lower costs and make good investment decisions – for wealth management and retirement. However, I would argue that trust is also playing a role here. Morningstar has about $300 billion in assets under management, and this is a lot of money for a lot of people – money they want to see protected well, as it is their money and life savings on the line.

License-Based Services

The picture is entirely different for license-based services. Here we are not talking about customer money but rather about data and analysis they need, and here the risk of switching to another competitor and provider of data might be much higher.

Belonging in this category are Morningstar Direct as well as PitchBook. And when looking at PitchBook, for example, we clearly see the problem. Revenue is still growing year-over-year, but growth rates are slowing down, and in the fourth quarter, reported growth was 7.7% and organic growth only 6.0%.

For Morningstar Direct, the growth rates are actually more stable and fluctuate around 6% year-over-year growth in the last few quarters. But the big risk is that customers might replace Morningstar Direct or PitchBook with other applications that provide similar functionality and maybe cheaper prices.

However, while investors and analysts seem to be rather concerned, management is still optimistic and responded to an investor question in December 2025:

Looking forward, the opportunity is twofold: First, we seek to expand our reach within existing accounts and capture remaining large AUM logos who aren’t our clients through targeted marketing and sales strategies. At the same time, PitchBook will continue to focus on adding new logos across small- and mid-sized firms in our core as well as adjacent client segments like corporates and service providers. Combined with opportunities to upsell and cross-sell within existing accounts, we believe these initiatives position us to sustain growth even as markets normalize. More broadly, we expect that expansion with existing large AUM accounts will continue to outpace growth from new logos even as both remain important growth drivers.

In the CEO Letter, Kunal Kapoor also points toward the private-markets ecosystem expanding and that PitchBook’s breadth and depth of firmographic information will generate an edge. And while I see a risk for GenAI applications being able to replace applications that analyze data and provide other information, I would once again point toward the difficulty of providing accurate information (similar to the index segment – see above). It's easy to analyze data and draw conclusions, and GenAI applications might perform very well here. But getting the data is much more difficult, and you probably have to turn toward a service like Morningstar and pay for the data.

Additionally, Morningstar can include GenAI applications in its offering – like PitchBook Navigator and Valuation Estimates. And – as already pointed out in my last article – Morningstar can also use AI to lower its cost to provide the necessary data. This trend is still visible in the headcount number that is getting lower and lower over time.

Losing Consistency

The biggest problem is the loss of consistency and predictability for the business. Morningstar is facing a similar problem as many other software businesses or companies like FactSet Research. These companies are relying heavily on subscriptions and are offering their products and services by charging monthly or annual fees. This led to predictable cash flows and predictable growth rates. These models seem to shift a little bit.

And why this is a problem for Morningstar becomes obvious when looking at the revenue by the three different types. The biggest part of revenue is stemming from license-based revenue, and these were predictable cash flows in the past. Morningstar will probably still see revenue from PitchBook growing over the years to come, but growth rates might fluctuate more – similar to the credit ratings business.

This is also the explanation why Morningstar's stock price has declined so steeply in the last few quarters – hand-in-hand with companies like FactSet. And it's also the explanation why Moody’s Corporation, S&P Global, or MSCI, Inc. (MSCI) were more stable. These companies are not only growing at a higher pace but also generate a bigger part of their revenue from other sources.

Intrinsic Value Calculation

Together with a declining stock price, the price-earnings ratio and the price free cash flow ratio declined as well. Right now, the stock is trading for 20.7 times earnings and for 17.6 times free cash flow. Of course, this is not extremely cheap, but if Morningstar is able to grow at least in the low-to-mid single digits, the business is at least fairly valued.

Using a discount cash flow calculation, we get to a similar result. As always, we're calculating with a discount rate of 10% (the minimum annual return we like to achieve) as well as 40.7 million diluted shares outstanding (the last reported number). The basis of our calculation can be the free cash flow of the last four quarters, which was $443 million.

This amount also seems very reasonable at this point, as Morningstar is reporting a free cash flow margin of 18.1% and therefore slightly below the long-term average of 19.4%. Now we have to determine what growth rates we should use here – and in a first cautious calculation, we assume 6% growth for the next ten years followed by 4% growth till perpetuity. This leads to an intrinsic value of $207.12 for Morningstar.

And if we're a little more optimistic, we can calculate with 9% growth for the next 10 years followed by 4% growth till perpetuity. All other assumptions being the same, we get an intrinsic value of $252.98, and the stock is clearly undervalued in this scenario.

Are We Too Cautious?

For the above, I calculate with 6% growth for the years to come, and we certainly can raise the question if these growth rates are reasonable. We should not ignore that revenue grew with a CAGR of 14.7% since 2001 (the oldest data available) and with a CAGR of 12.0% in the last 10 years. Earnings per share grew 13.5% annually since 2005 (I excluded the previous year due to the company either reporting a loss or a very low EPS that would disturb the numbers) and with a CAGR of 11.5% in the last 10 years.

Analysts also expect a much higher pace for the next few years and assuming earnings per share to grow with a CAGR of 17.7% in the next few years until fiscal 2028. Additionally, we see margins improving again in the last few years. Gross margin and operating margin improved in the last few years, which is also a good sign.

Additionally, the company is reporting a return on invested capital of 14.59% right now – which is in line with the long-term average and indicating an economic moat around the business.

While all these are arguments for higher growth rates in the years to come, we should not ignore the risk of AI mentioned above and the risks of another recession. When looking at the chart, we see Morningstar being hit hard during the Great Financial Crisis.

Conclusion

In my opinion, Morningstar is clearly a “Buy” at this point and undervalued. The AI fears should not be ignored, but the stock was pushed down too far and only some parts of Morningstar’s business are at risk. To be honest, I would rather prefer to buy Moody’s or S&P Global for a huge discount, as these businesses are better than Morningstar – but so far, both stocks have declined only a little and are not extremely undervalued.

In the end it comes down to one of Warren Buffett’s famous quotes. We have to decide whether we want to buy a fair company for a wonderful price (Morningstar) or a wonderful business for a fair price (this might be Moody’s Corporation or S&P Global). This is the reason why I remain to the sidelines – I like to wait how the current software and license-business crash will play out and what bargains might be created. But in the end, Morningstar is probably a solid “Buy” at this point and will be a good long-term investment.

And while I see Moody’s or S&P Global are probably better companies, we should not ignore that Morningstar clearly outperformed the overall market during the last 20 years – even when including the 50% decline in the recent past.

data.title: Grupo Aeroportuario del Centro Norte: I'm Not Worried About Oil Prices
data.description:
Mexican airport operator Grupo Aeroportuario del Centro Norte, S.A.B. de C.V. (NASDAQ:OMAB) had been on a great run, with shares rising more than 50% over the past year up through February.

However, a combination of negative developments has caused OMAB stock to experience a quick 20% pullback off the recent highs. I'm a long-time Centro Norte shareholder and have written extensively about the company here at Seeking Alpha over the years. Here's my assessment of the latest risk factors and why I'm not losing any sleep as a shareholder today.

Potential Risks: Cartel Issues & Higher Oil Prices

You may be wondering about the recent Mexican military operation against a prominent cartel leader and the subsequent violence that ensued. Will it have a significant impact on Centro Norte? My quick take is that no, it will not. If you want more detail as to why, I covered that incident in a recent article on fellow Mexican airport peer Grupo Aeroportuario del Pacifico (PAC).

Pacifico is the operator of the two airports, Puerto Vallarta and Guadalajara, which were at the heart of those headlines. Centro Norte's key airports, by contrast, are further geographically removed from where the recent issues were centered.

And, for what it's worth, Centro Norte's CEO, Ricardo Duenas, responded directly to a question on the issue on the company's latest conference call. Duenas stated that:

"We did see on Sunday during the event, a few cancellations from Guadalajara and Puerto Vallarta Airport. There were some yesterday, but today is operating normally, and it is not something that [...] we believe will have an impact in our numbers."

I'm willing to revisit this subject if subsequent traffic data comes in soft. But, based on what we know now, I simply don't see this being something that is going to have any material impact on Mexican air traffic at any airports other than Puerto Vallarta and Guadalajara, both of which are Pacifico rather than Centro Norte properties.

Switching gears, I also see folks fretting about higher oil prices as a potential risk factor for the airport operators. And, as far as that goes, I'd grant that jet fuel is a significant near-term risk to the airlines themselves. Take a look at the US Global Jets ETF (JETS), which has retreated more than 10% in recent weeks:

However, there's a fundamental difference in business models here. Airlines are highly operating companies with massive fixed costs (unionized pilots and crew, aircraft leases, maintenance, etc.). They generate huge ticket sales figures but run tiny profit margins on those sales. A short-term spike in operating costs and/or plunge in revenues can cause airlines to become insolvent incredibly quickly. Even seemingly well-run airlines that previously had solid stock price performance, like Spirit Airlines, to give one example, can lose their way and destroy shareholder value in a hurry.

But the Mexican airport operators, such as Centro Norte, run 60%+ EBITDA margins. And they don't use much debt either. Over the past 12 months, for example, Centro Norte generated $496 million of operating income and had just $70 million of net interest expenses against that. The company can easily shoulder a recession or tourism-industry-specific downturn in traffic without having any balance sheet issues. And ultimately, even if some airlines do go bust during an economic shock, generally, the airplanes are simply repainted and then leased out to a new start-up airline that replaces the failed carrier's capacity. To give an OMAB-specific example of this, Mexico's then second-largest carrier, Mexicana, went bust in 2010, and all the lost capacity was replaced within two years.

It's possible that if the Iran issues are not quickly addressed, we'll see higher ticket prices and thus some demand destruction later in 2026. However, I'm highly skeptical that oil prices will remain elevated for more than a couple of months due to the current disruption, so any negative impact is likely to be transitory.

And, for Centro Norte specifically, it's the best-positioned of the four LatAm airport operators for this sort of shock. Its bread-and-butter is traffic for business-focused cities with large manufacturing hubs such as Monterrey and Juarez.

If Asian manufacturers face an energy shock due to the Strait of Hormuz issues, that makes it more appealing for U.S. companies to order goods from nearby Mexico rather than alternatives such as Japan, China, or South Korea.

Northern Mexico is located close to California, Texas, and other significant demand centers. If shipping costs go up, Mexico wins a greater share of that business due to its proximity to the U.S. market, thus driving an impulse for more activity in industrial cities where OMAB operates airports. This positions Centro Norte better than, say, Grupo Aeroportuario del Sureste (ASR), which is reliant on Cancun tourism flows rather than business traffic for a much larger chunk of its revenues.

Valuation & Dividend Policy

While traders have sold off OMAB stock over the past month, the bullish case remains solidly in place. The company is set to enjoy a contractual 6.9% real (protected from inflation) increase in its allowable rates for 2026. Combine that with the company's projected low-to-mid single-digit increase in airport traffic this year, and in my view, it's likely that the company should be on pace for another year of double-digit top-line revenue growth, especially if the Mexican Peso remains on an upswing compared to the U.S. Dollar.

Analysts disagree with me slightly; they see 8% revenue growth this year as of this writing:

Even with the analyst consensus figures, however, the stock seems attractively priced at a starting 12x P/E ratio that moves to 9x over the next two years. Usually, you'd expect to see a higher valuation for this sort of logistics asset with a natural geographic monopoly that is posting such solid and consistent top-line growth.

Another key aspect of that steady earnings growth is that management returns the lion's share of free cash flow back to its shareholders.

Centro Norte, like many Latin American companies, does not pay a fixed quarterly dividend amount. Rather, the dividend policy is based on profitability, with the dividend quickly adjusting to the firm's most recent operating results.

Centro Norte has been a tremendous, albeit lumpy, dividend growth story. The firm was paying about $1.50/share per year in dividends prior to the pandemic. The dividend was, understandably, suspended at the onset of the global travel stoppage. But, once the crisis passed, management realized that it had an unusual amount of cash that had piled up on the balance sheet and gave investors a massive special dividend in 2022.

The dividend has bounced up and down since then but appears to be settling in around $5/share per year, or roughly triple what Centro Norte was paying prior to the pandemic:

Putting these elements together, OMAB stock is offering a more than 4.5% starting dividend yield. And, if the future is anything like the past, the firm's consistent 10% or so topline growth should translate into a similar annualized dividend growth rate. 10-12% annualized dividend growth off a current 4.6% base adds up to a rather generous income stream over the years.

I've been an OMAB shareholder since 2016 and have held shares through numerous panics, including the Trump election win of 2016, the AMLO election win in 2018, the pandemic, and the 2023 contract renegotiation scare. My first OMAB share purchases were in the $40s, and I've gotten back more than $25/share in cumulative dividends on those initial lots of stock that I own. I share this perspective because it emphasizes how these are high-value logistic assets that offer a steady "toll road" style of profits and ensuing dividends. This sort of business is easier to hold during inevitable economic jitters and particular negative developments such as pandemics or oil shocks due to the large recurring income element.

OMAB Stock Bottom Line

Of the three publicly traded Mexican airport operators, Centro Norte is currently the one I am most bullish on. To be clear, I have significant holdings in all three, and my opinion on their timeliness changes periodically as the macroeconomic landscape shifts. I quite like Sureste's recent deal to pick up the Brazilian airport package. And Pacifico seemed like it was on course for a favorable 2026 before the recent cartel issues clipped its growth outlook at Guadalajara and Puerto Vallarta a bit.

With the current headwinds potentially facing tourism, both globally and in Mexico specifically, I see Sureste and Pacifico as being more vulnerable to a potential growth deceleration over the next 6-12 months. Centro Norte, by contrast, is least exposed to that risk, and if anything, it might benefit at the margins if a global energy shock forces more manufacturing capacity to move from Asia to North America.

Finally, on an EV/EBITDA basis, with the correction over the past month, OMAB shares have returned to 11x. This is below the 10-year median, though not by a huge margin:

Perhaps in a prolonged Iran war scenario or some other such economic setback, OMAB stock could return to 8-9x EBITDA, where it traded in 2024. However, the stock has spent a lot of time in the 10-12x range over the years, and it used to (prior to 2016) sell as high as 15x from time to time. So, the current valuation, while not quite the deep value it was two years ago, remains perfectly attractive. I'm sticking with a buy rating on the stock today.

data.title: Arista Networks: All Ready To Storm To New Highs (Upgrade)
data.description:
Arista Networks: Networking Is The Next Choke Point

For those of you who thought that the AI boom was mostly about NVIDIA Corporation (NVDA), a timely article penned by CEO Jensen Huang provides important insights into elaborating why the AI infrastructure buildout is expected to broaden and proliferate across the economy.

In the now-famous “AI is a five-layer cake” analogy, he provides us with a pictorial representation of where Nvidia’s opportunity set currently is and where it has also expanded into, capturing economics across the layers that align with its core competencies. And one of them is arguably in the networking space, right?

Within the five-layer cake, Nvidia enunciates why networking falls within the infrastructure labeling, but isn’t the only thing that belongs to that layer, but nonetheless is pivotal to where Nvidia believes it is competitive and well positioned to gain share and expand its growth pillars:

The above chips are infrastructure. This includes land, power delivery, cooling, construction, networking, and the systems that orchestrate tens of thousands of processors into one machine. These systems are AI factories. They are not designed to store information. They are designed to manufacture intelligence. - Jensen Huang on “AI is a five-layer cake.”

So the AI chip race that has been dominated by none other than Jensen Huang and his team is expected to feature networking more prominently from here, as the company faces questions on the potential for market share losses to custom chip archrivals like Broadcom Inc. (AVGO) or even its merchant chip peer, Advanced Micro Devices, Inc. (AMD). It is definitely not a coincidence that Nvidia wants to convince the market that networking has become the most important growth driver, taking over from its compute dominance.

While networking accounted for just over 20% of its compute revenue base, it grew by an astounding 270% over the past one year. Therefore, Nvidia’s ability to bundle its networking platform together with its GPUs and CUDA software libraries is a formidable go-to-market strategy that could have significant implications for Arista Networks, Inc. (ANET).

As for ANET, while it’s still the Ethernet market leader, Nvidia’s rapid growth has really caught the eye of the market. I guess I don’t have to impress upon you that the stock has underperformed the S&P 500 (SP500) since my last update in September 2025. Back then, while I was impressed with the growth profile of ANET, aligning closely with the pace of the AI buildout, valuation concerns held me back from assigning a more optimistic rating to the stock. While the underperformance since that update is not glaring, it’s still a noteworthy development, considering that the stock is still up more than 65% in the past year.

So it does seem like investors are considering possibly even tougher competition against Nvidia, even though I must highlight that management did put up a courageous front, in reminding us that networking is a broad spectrum that Nvidia doesn’t fully address with its products and solutions, of which Arista is well positioned to capture the economics:

They've (Nvidia) been very successful at the back-end networks that interconnect GPUs. But our Cloud Titan customers, certainly including Meta, have much more broad and diverse networking needs than that. So if you're talking about the backbone, talk about the data center spine. You're talking about the access networks. That's a place where I think Arista is well differentiated, and it's very strong. - Arista Networks, Inc. Presents at Morgan Stanley Technology, Media & Telecom Conference 2026 Transcript.

Arista Networks: Growth Expected To Stay Resilient

I think management provided clear insights into why Arista’s hardware/software integration offers customers a clear differentiation lever over peers, and Arista’s consistent revenue growth corroborates why the firm lifted its guidance to more than 25% for FY2026, as the AI infrastructure buildout continues at an unrelenting pace. As a result, I think ANET bulls will want to remind us that networking could be the next big chokepoint in the AI boom, as it has since moved from chips to storage, spurring the memory stocks to spectacular outperformance that surpassed even my most optimistic imagination.

And Arista is arguably firmly established on scale-up, scale-out, and scale across workloads, so it’s not difficult to envisage that the company’s networking technologies will remain dominant, which have already been validated by its main hyperscaler customers like Meta and Microsoft. Hence, I expect Arista’s solutions to be front and center as the AI data centers expand to “massive scale,” benefiting from Arista’s hardware (scalable architecture), and enabled by its EOS capabilities for “intelligent real-time load balancing that accounts for actual network utilization to uniformly distribute traffic and avoid flow collisions.”

Therefore, I think the six-month consolidation in its price action appears ready for the next advancing phase, once the market gets past the initial hesitance over its well-deserved premium valuation. As AI clusters become increasingly prolific, as well as larger, high-quality networking technologies will undoubtedly feature even more prominently in big tech’s discussion.

Nevertheless, I think that the threat from white box solutions could still hamper a more aggressive bullish rerating in ANET, eating into its margins profile. Yet, the simplicity and scalability of Arista’s software platform should help allay fears, as hyperscalers still rely on ANET’s domain expertise, although we cannot rule out the need for big tech to optimize their CapEx spending with less expensive white box solutions if their homemade software platform manages to improve over time, which could lead to higher potential share losses to these providers.

Buy Or Sell ANET Stock?

For a stock that’s valued at 38x forward earnings, I will be disingenuous if I mention that ANET stock isn't expensive. It’s still well over the 21.8x sector median, expressing the high level of optimism that the market has baked into its growth momentum. Yet, the wisdom of the market suggests to me that buyers are not unduly worried about whether ANET could deliver in the current context, where AI networking has proven to be a highly lucrative opportunity, as we saw with Nvidia’s rapid growth rates.

While competition has always been intense, I think ANET’s upgraded outlook, coupled with the bullish print from its price action, corroborates my decision to view its prospects with a more optimistic lens than before. And when I consider that ANET’s valuation has reverted closer to its 10-year average of 35x, from a high of 50x back in September, I think the market is setting up very nicely for a well-deserved advancing phase once the consolidation comes to a conclusive end.

Rating: Upgrade to Buy.

data.title: Comcast Looks Intriguing Here, But History Remains A Concern
data.description:
There are a number of ways to make the bull case for Comcast Corporation (CMCSA) stock here. Shares have already bounced 26% from a 10-year low reached in late October, but on a consolidated basis they remain cheap relative to earnings and cash flow. The core Residential & Connectivity Platforms segment, which drove ~two-thirds of Adjusted EBITDA in 2025, offers a turnaround case. Comcast is revamping its go-to-market strategy and pricing in a bid to recapture customers lost to competition.

In the remaining part of the business, there are potential "hidden asset" arguments for assets like Universal Studios and the theme park business, both of which ostensibly should receive far higher valuations than does Comcast as a whole. That in turn might suggest a "sum of the parts" case, which too argues for solid upside for Comcast on paper. And ostensibly, the arguments for the two sides of the business should work together: a scenario where sentiment improves toward the core broadband offering should see investors also give greater focus to (and have stronger confidence in) the rest of the portfolio.

It's a tempting case, certainly. But that's almost part of the problem. The history of the past 15 years or so shows that these tempting cases so rarely play out. Consumer-facing businesses dealing with secular challenges have seen their valuations come in and thus seem to provide opportunities, particularly relative to historical valuation. That trend has held across multiple industries: packaged food, alcoholic beverages, legacy media, and retail, to name just a few.

Very few of those cases have actually worked out. Most legacy businesses that manage secular change, unsurprisingly, have not been able to navigate well. It's possible Comcast is an exception to the trend, but at least for now it seems a bit too early to bet aggressively that it will be.

The Comcast Turnaround

Looking to 2025 results, Comcast stock looks exceptionally attractive. Shares trade at about 5.4x last year's Adjusted EBITDA, and more importantly, barely 5x 2025 free cash flow. Tax benefits related to the spin-off of Versant (VSNT) depress the latter's multiple, but even adjusting for those benefits, Comcast is still priced for declining profit going forward.

There's certainly an agreement that the outlook is too pessimistic. 2025 performance was soft but hardly disastrous: revenue was flat, and Adjusted EBITDA declined less than 2%. The Peacock streaming service is still running losses—per earnings releases, Adjusted EBITDA for the platform in 2025 was negative $1.08 billion—and simply getting that to break even adds something like 5-6% to tax-normalized free cash flow. ($800 million after-tax improvement on a current ~$15 billion FCF base.)

So, very simply, the case here is basically that if Comcast can deliver stable free cash flow, the stock potentially has double-digit annualized upside. Roughly speaking, even a 10x P/FCF multiple suggests three- to four-year total returns of 60% at least (a ~$160B market cap would be a 44% increase, while the dividend adds ~4% a year and buybacks/deleveraging potentially more). In a world where Comcast can convince the market its business has improved, triple-digit total returns absolutely are on the proverbial table.

And the best way to convince the market is to get the RCP segment fixed. Here, too, it's not as if results have been disastrous: Adjusted EBITDA increased each year from 2021 to 2024 (at a ~2.8% annualized rate) before declining last year. More pressure is coming in 2026 as the company reworks pricing and promotions—but the hope is that those efforts can jumpstart growth and fend off competition from 'overbuilders' and fixed wireless offerings.

As co-chief executive officer Mike Cavanagh put it at a conference this month, "We had pricing that was less transparent and more harder [sic] to engage with for the consumer with more difficulty getting product onboarded than the new competition, particularly fixed wireless brought." Basically, for lower-usage customers (and I saw this firsthand with a family member who switched away from a different broadband provider), moving to fixed wireless was a simple choice. Pricing was lower (at least upfront), and installation was/is pretty much painless.

By streamlining pricing, offering a new five-year price guarantee, and making the overall process less complicated, Comcast thinks it can win in those markets. And, as Cavanagh has noted, the fact that it held essential monopolies in so many markets means it has exceptionally granular data on the customers who left for fixed wireless or fiber and thus can put together offers to win those customers back.

For higher-usage customers, meanwhile, broadband should remain superior to fixed wireless, and there too better marketing and simplified pricing should help Comcast compete. The bull case for CMCSA and Charter Communications (CHTR) before the full arrival of fixed wireless was that growing data demands (streaming video, Internet of Things, etc.) would keep them insulated from wireless competition, and for some users it seems likely that outlook will hold.

Comcast also is offering wireless plans of its own, which offer the potential to eventually add profit while also strengthening retention in broadband. Many of the lines in service now have been given for free; as customers move toward paid plans (later this year), Comcast should see revenue growth. At the very least, costs to serve free users should go away (though management has said the offering already is profitable), reversing some of the profit hit expected for the segment this year.

Beyond Broadband

Meanwhile, there are valuable assets beyond the core business. The Theme Parks segment generated just over $3 billion in Adjusted EBITDA in 2025 (up 25% from 2019 levels, which admittedly is fine but not spectacular). Valuing that business is tough (the same problem holds for the SOTP cases for Disney (DIS)), but at a 10x-plus multiple, it seems more than reasonable. (Six Flags (FUN) is above 8x, and that company is a mess.) That covers ~30% of the current market capitalization and provides growth opportunities even if connectivity remains stagnant.

As a number of analysts have noted, the Studios business has real value as well. Adjusted EBITDA was just over $1 billion in 2025, but the bidding war for Warner Bros. Discovery (WBD) suggests the Universal assets would be exceptionally valuable to the right owner. The Media segment does offset some of this potential upside (profits are declining, and even with the weaker networks shunted off to Versant, the outlook is not particularly bright), but overall, a rough SOTP also suggests a path toward double-digit annualized gains:

  • RCP: $22 billion in 2026 EBITDA (-15%) at 5.5x (CHTR is at 5.7x 2026E) gets to $121 billion;
  • Business: $5.7 billion in 2026 EBITDA (flat) at 6x (the trend has been better than residential), for ~$34 billion;
  • Media: $2.9 billion in 2026 EBITDA (-10%) at 3x (see peers), for ~$9 billion;
  • Theme Parks: $3 billion-plus in 2026 EBITDA (small growth) at 12x, for ~$38 billion;
  • Studios: $1.3 billion in 2026 EBITDA (strong slate should drive higher profits), at 15x (trophy asset?), getting to ~$20 billion.

That admittedly rough (but often conservative) SOTP puts enterprise value at about $222 billion, which would get the market cap to $132 billion. That suggests about 19% gains to a CMCSA stock price of $36. And there is upside there, particularly if the RCP turnaround can hold (note that our model uses 2026 profits, which are ostensibly somewhat depressed).

Can This Case Work?

So on paper, there is an argument that the rally from October lows can continue. And while "on paper" is a huge caveat there (in investing in general and particularly for this kind of name), forced to choose, I'd rather own CMCSA than short it. New blood in the top ranks can probably help, particularly given that the Roberts family had run Comcast for over six decades. There's no doubt room for improvement in terms of execution, and there is perhaps the potential for further financial engineering after the VSNT spin. (That spin has not gone that badly, given that it was clearly why Comcast wanted that business off the books.)

But at the same time, it's worth reiterating how much history works against the bull case here. For essentially the entire 21st century, few companies have been able to avoid being disrupted and outcompeted by new entrants. And, again, that's particularly true in established, consumer-facing industries. There have been strong cases on paper for AT&T (T) and General Mills (GIS); for the Detroit automakers, and for so many retailers. So few of these plays, with familiar names but a risk that their best days have passed, have worked. (The one obvious success is General Electric (GE); I'm likely forgetting at least a couple of others.)

And given how much pressure is on such a huge proportion of EBITDA (Media plus RCP is three-quarters of total profit), the core risk here is that history repeats once again. The combination of two competitors keeps a lid on pricing. Meanwhile, costs aren't stable because, at the same time, Comcast needs constant network improvements to keep pace. (This is basically the AT&T/Verizon (VZ) problem in wireless.) Theme Parks is good but not spectacular or big enough to offset profit declines or create shareholder value in a transaction. CMCSA maybe provides some income but underperforms the market.

Again, there are reasons to think that Comcast can chart a different path than many companies in similar situations. But those reasons, for now, remain relatively speculative (particularly in terms of the turnaround efforts in broadband), and this may be a case where it's wiser to own at $35 with signs of success than at $30 when the outlook remains uncertain. Comcast's strategy can work, but putting money behind it requires believing that it likely will work—and we're just not quite there yet.

data.title: After A Massive Rally, The Easy Money May Be Gone - Prologis
data.description:
Prologis, Inc. (PLD) currently sits in our neutral range for valuation. I think that’s fair. Here are a few factors to consider:

  • Shares (at $132.05 intraday) trade at 27.63x forward AFFO consensus estimates of $4.80. This is on the high end for REITs presently.

  • Shares are about 47.8% above their lowest close over the last 52 weeks.

  • Shares are about 7% under their highest close over the last 52 weeks (set in early March 2026).

  • Dividend yield is at 3% with about 4.9% guidance for growth in Core FFO per share excluding net promote income this year.

  • We want to pay strong attention to the growth. However, we are focused on long-term growth rates while acknowledging that short-term growth rates can have a large impact on the multiple.

  • Prologis has rallied all the way from our “Strong Buy” range to our “neutral” range. We currently have a neutral rating on PLD. (For reference, our last article was here).

Here’s a chart showing the historical price and multiples:

Using the share price or the forward AFFO multiple, we saw the valuation move up quite a bit.

Prologis has delivered a strong rally over the last year, with shares climbing substantially in roughly the last 11 months. That performance has pushed the valuation higher and took our valuation from strong buy to our neutral area. With the stock already reflecting some optimism, it becomes important to evaluate the latest operating results and the outlook for continued growth.

Prologis Core FFO And AFFO Results And Guidance

Prologis results:

  • Core FFO of $1.44 matched estimates of $1.44. Excluding net promote income, $1.46 would beat estimates.

  • AFFO per share of $1.20 beat estimates of $1.15 by $.05.

Here’s the big chart:

We also track the guidance for Core FFO Ex Net Promote Income because I find that value is more useful for reflecting the performance of the real estate portfolio:

Note: We could use the term “Net Promote Expense.” For simplicity, I list it as “income” even if we have a negative value. Over long periods the value will usually be positive.

Looking To 2026

  • Consensus estimate was $6.13.

  • Guidance for Core FFO was $6.10 (lower by $.03).

  • Guidance for Core FFO Ex Net Promote Income was $6.15 (higher by $.02).

That’s implying growth continues at about 5%. Absent any negative shocks, we may see PLD beat the original guidance as they did last year. For 2025 they ended up about 1.5% above the original guidance.

Guidance for same-store net operating income is important also. It is one of the major factors we consider when evaluating long-term growth rates and the appeal of the underlying real estate.

That’s slightly faster growth in cash NOI than PLD saw in 2025.

General Notes

The major criteria continue to look good.

Average occupancy guidance has a midpoint of 95.25%, similar to the best quarter for PLD in 2025. This matches commentary from much earlier (several quarters ago) about when occupancy was expected to hit the low point and the rebound we would most likely see.

Rent change on renewals (leasing spread) is coming down but is still quite solid. If this trend continues, it could pressure same-store net operating income. But we’re still seeing healthy performance for PLD.

Interest Rates

Like many REITs with operations around the globe, PLD has utilized lower interest rates in Europe for a greater portion of their debt recently. This mitigates part of the pressure from higher rates. They also have a reasonably long weighted maturity on their debts at 8.5 years. However, long-duration (like 10-year Treasury) interest rates are currently trending higher in the United States. That is generally negative for valuation.

Over the last several days, we’ve also seen short-term rates jumping higher.

Data Centers

We may see PLD putting about 40% of their new development into data centers (based on the PLD Q4 2025 earnings call).

I’ve mentioned before that data centers can be built at fairly high yields, which is quite attractive. It creates a potential challenge down the road from excessive development, but it makes them attractive development projects today. This is still a minor portion of PLD’s portfolio, but they’ve evaluated the land in their portfolio and found many cases where they can optimize the value by building data centers (instead of industrial). This can also be used for attracting investor capital into the development funds Prologis manages. I like this method of exposure to data centers much more than I would like owning data center REITs. PLD is still an overwhelmingly industrial REIT with a great balance sheet and expertise in development.

My Expectations

I’m inclined to think PLD will slightly outperform guidance so long as the economy doesn’t go off the rails. That’s pretty good. They keep the balance sheet strong and deliver steady growth over time. The performance of the underlying portfolio backs the growth in AFFO per share. However, valuation has been pushing quite a bit higher. I’m not attracted to the current multiple.

With occupancy averaging about 95% over 2025, PLD has some room to grow there. Management is great at forecasting global development and demand levels. I expect them to be right. However, I find the projections they provide typically are usually designed to be slightly cautious, so they are less likely to have something “go wrong.” Management calls for global warehouse occupancy to increase slightly over the year, with rent growth emerging towards the end of the year.

Conclusion

AFFO per share solidly beat estimates, but AFFO can be a bit volatile because of the timing for maintenance capex. In this case, I think AFFO introduces more volatility for PLD than it does for some of the non-industrial REITs.

Guidance was roughly on par with estimates, depending on how we adjust for the negative $.05 related to net promote income. I see that as a non-issue. So I don’t think anything here should land as a positive or negative surprise.

Recent Trade

I believe it is important to disclose trading activity and to provide evidence for trades. If investors don’t track their positions, they can end up with some delusional ideas about their performance. We recently closed out the vast majority of our position in Prologis. The trade was provided to subscribers with a real-time trade alert:

To enhance transparency, I also believe it is important to include screenshots showing confirmation of a trade. Some people say there is no reason to do that. I do it for two reasons:

  1. Many investors have told me that it is easier for them to be clear about my outlook when they can see the trade.

  2. It establishes a definitive statement about the actual execution. Typos happen. Miscommunication happens. The screenshot prevents miscommunication. Either that trade happened exactly as shown, or the analyst faked the screenshot and should be held to account.

Therefore, here is the screenshot:

Here are the returns on the shares I sold:
